output:
# Heterogeneity-Conditional Learning-Rate Bounds for Personalized Federated Learning: The MONOBAND Gap and the RHEOSTAT Method

A research-idea proposal in the 5-question (5q) format. The proposal reframes the "dynamic bound of learning rate" device used in personalized federated learning (pFL) from a purely temporal optimization stabilizer into a per-client, heterogeneity-conditional personalization lever, while preserving a provable, zero-regression exact-collapse identity back to the base method.

Notation note: all symbols are written in plain ASCII. Greek letters are spelled out (alpha, beta, eta, theta, kappa, lambda, mu, sigma). Inequalities use "<=" and ">=". Em-dashes are written as " - ".

---

## Section 1. Background and Current State of the Art

### 1.1 Federated learning and statistical heterogeneity

Federated learning (FL) trains a shared model across many clients by repeatedly downloading a global model, training locally on private data, uploading local updates, and aggregating them on a server, all without centralizing raw data [1]. The defining obstacle is statistical heterogeneity: client data is not independent and identically distributed (Non-IID) and is unbalanced in quantity [19], [20], [23]. Under heterogeneity, a single global model produced by naive averaging generalizes poorly to the individual clients whose local distributions diverge most from the federated aggregate [19], [20].

The canonical method, FedAvg [1], averages local models into one global model and degrades under Non-IID and unbalanced data [20]. A first family of remedies stabilizes the global objective without abandoning the single-model goal. FedProx [2] adds a proximal term that anchors each local update to the current global model with a global coefficient mu. SCAFFOLD [3] introduces control variates to correct client drift, at the cost of communicating extra control state. FedNova [17] normalizes heterogeneous local progress to remove objective inconsistency. FedMA [16] aggregates layer-wise with matched averaging. FAVOR [15] selects a subset of clients each round with a deep reinforcement-learning policy built on double Q-learning [27]. FedPAQ [21] reduces communication via periodic averaging and quantization. All of these still target a single global model and inherit the fundamental limit that one model cannot fit every divergent client [19].

### 1.2 Personalized federated learning (pFL)

Personalized FL accepts that each client may need its own model and asks how the federation should share statistical strength without forcing a single solution. Three broad pFL families are now standard. (i) Regularization / interpolation methods couple a per-client model to the global model with a scalar strength: pFedMe [8] uses a Moreau-envelope regularization weight lambda; Ditto [11] regularizes a personalized model toward the global solution with a global lambda; APFL [10] mixes local and global predictors with an adaptively updated, per-client mixing coefficient alpha_i. (ii) Meta-learning and representation methods learn a good initialization or a shared representation that each client fine-tunes: Per-FedAvg [9] learns a MAML-style initialization with inner/outer learning rates; FedRep [22] shares a representation and personalizes the head. (iii) Architecture / weighting methods adapt what is shared: pFedHN [12] generates client models from a hypernetwork; PartialFed [13] personalizes which parameters are loaded versus locally initialized; FedFomo [14] combines other clients' models with first-order weights. Surveys frame these as the dominant responses to heterogeneity [19], [23].

A recurring structural fact unites most of these families: the personalization "amount" is controlled by one or two scalars (a regularization weight lambda or mu, a meta learning rate) that are tuned for the federation as a whole and then applied identically to every client. APFL [10] is a partial exception, adapting its mixing weight per client, but from a local optimization signal rather than an observable heterogeneity covariate, and never at the learning-rate-bound layer (a distinction developed in Question 4).

### 1.3 Adaptive optimization and the dynamic bound of the learning rate

Orthogonal to pFL, the optimization community studied how to set the learning rate adaptively. Adam [6] scales each coordinate by a running second moment; AMSGrad [7] fixes a non-convergence case in Adam. AdaBound [5] - the method the source Introduction references as a "dynamic bound of learning rate" - clips Adam's per-coordinate step into a time-dependent interval whose lower and upper bounds both converge to a final SGD step size eta_inf, so training begins adaptive (fast, Adam-like) and ends stable (SGD-like). In FL specifically, FedAdam and the FedOpt family [4] place adaptive optimizers on the SERVER side of aggregation. Federated hyperparameter optimization such as FedEx [26] searches for good client hyperparameters (including learning rates) by weight-sharing search over a shared configuration.

The Introduction situates the present work in the intersection of these lines: personalized FL methods that use a dynamic bound of the learning rate to control how each client moves from an adaptive regime to a stable shared regime during local training.

### 1.4 Evaluation conventions

Non-IID FL is benchmarked on CIFAR-10/100 [25] partitioned by a Dirichlet(alpha) concentration that controls label skew [28], and on naturally partitioned LEAF datasets such as FEMNIST and Shakespeare [24]. The near-universal headline metric is pooled (mean) accuracy across clients. Transfer-learning intuitions about feature reusability [29] motivate why a shared backbone helps even under skew.

### 1.5 Where the field stands

The state of the art has produced (a) better global optimization (FedProx, SCAFFOLD, FedNova, FedOpt/FedAdam), (b) explicit personalization knobs (pFedMe, Ditto, APFL, Per-FedAvg, FedRep, pFedHN, PartialFed, FedFomo), and (c) adaptive learning-rate schedules with dynamic bounds (AdaBound, FedAdam). What it has NOT produced is any method in which the dynamic learning-rate bound itself is conditioned on each client's measured statistical heterogeneity. The bound adapts to the OPTIMIZATION signal (per-coordinate gradient scale) and evolves with TIME, but it is the same schedule for a near-IID client and for the most skewed client in the federation. This proposal targets exactly that unexamined assumption.

---

## Section 2. Key Findings and Analysis

This section states the analytical core that the proposal in Section 3 formalizes. It names the gap (MONOBAND), the method (RHEOSTAT), the design principle (exact collapse), and the novelty wedge (C1-C4).

### 2.1 Finding 1 - The MONOBAND gap is a representational impossibility, not a tuning error

In a pFL method built on a dynamic learning-rate bound, the local optimizer at every client clips its per-coordinate step into a shrinking interval [eta_l(t), eta_u(t)] that funnels the step from an adaptive range toward a shared terminal step eta_inf [5]. The schedule that governs how fast and how tightly this funnel closes - call its controlling quantity the bound-tightening (or, inversely, the adaptive-retention) parameter - is a SINGLE GLOBAL SCALAR. We name this gap MONOBAND: a Monolithic, heterogeneity-blind Bound-band applied identically to all clients.

The key analytical claim is that a global scalar is representationally INCAPABLE of expressing the per-client optimal retention, because that optimum is client-conditional. A highly Non-IID client (large divergence from the federation) has an idiosyncratic local loss geometry; clamping it early onto a terminal step eta_inf that was calibrated for the federation under-fits its own minimum. A near-IID client aligns with the shared geometry; keeping it adaptive (wide bounds) too long injects avoidable variance and forfeits the stability of the shared terminal regime. One scalar cannot be simultaneously loose enough for the skewed tail and tight enough for the aligned core. This is the same structural defect identified across prior instantiations of this template (a single global mixup rate, a single regularization weight, a single smoothing intensity): a scalar where a function of an observable covariate is required.

### 2.2 Finding 2 - The harm is SILENT under pooled accuracy (metric blindness)

The reason MONOBAND has persisted is that its damage is invisible to the field's headline metric. Over-tightening the skewed tail LOWERS its accuracy; under-tightening the near-IID core LOWERS its accuracy by a smaller per-client amount but the core is the majority. In the pooled mean these two errors partially cancel, and a global scalar tuned to maximize the mean sits at the compromise that hides the tail collapse. The harm therefore (i) is SILENT under pooled accuracy, (ii) SCALES with the degree of heterogeneity in the federation, and (iii) VANISHES under IID data, where every client's optimal retention equals the global one. Detecting MONOBAND requires divergence-stratified evaluation - accuracy as a function of each client's heterogeneity - which is not standard practice. Metric blindness is the binding reason the gap is open.

### 2.3 Finding 3 - The fix is a structured, monotone, covariate-conditional map: RHEOSTAT

We name the method RHEOSTAT, by analogy to a rheostat that continuously regulates a rate, in contrast to the base method's single fixed setting. RHEOSTAT replaces the global scalar with a per-client retention factor b(d_i) that is a STRUCTURED MONOTONE function of an OBSERVABLE heterogeneity covariate d_i - a shared low-dimensional functional form (a single-slope map by default), optionally refined by HIERARCHICAL PARTIAL POOLING across heterogeneity bins so that data-poor clients shrink toward the global default. Concretely, b(d_i) = clamp(1 + g_theta(d_i)) with g_theta monotone non-decreasing and g_theta(0) = 0, and the bound time-constant is stretched by b(d_i) so that more heterogeneous clients retain the adaptive (wide-bound) regime longer. The covariate d_i is the divergence between client i's locally adapted model (or its empirical label profile) and the downloaded global model (or global label prior), computed CLIENT-SIDE from quantities the client already holds and consumed locally inside the optimizer, so the deployable default adds zero per-round communication and touches only the local-optimizer layer - not the per-round model-update exchange, the aggregation step, the model architecture, or the privacy contract.

### 2.4 Finding 4 - Exact collapse gives a zero-regression do-no-harm floor

The decisive design property is that RHEOSTAT contains the base method as an exact special case. Setting theta = 0 makes g_theta identically zero, hence b(d_i) = 1 for every client, hence the per-client bound schedule equals the base global schedule, hence the local update is reproduced step-for-step. We gate this with E_collapse = max over clients and rounds of the per-update parameter difference between RHEOSTAT at theta=0 and the base method, and require E_collapse <= 1e-6 as a continuous-integration assertion. Because the identity holds by construction (an exact algebraic zero, g_theta(0)=0 with theta=0), there is no floating-point drift to accumulate. This makes RHEOSTAT shadow-deployable with a do-no-harm DEFAULT: at theta = 0 it reproduces the base method exactly, so the covariate-blind default is a fairness floor rather than a risk, and any active (theta > 0) configuration is adopted only when held-out validation shows it does not regress pooled accuracy.

### 2.5 Finding 5 - The novelty wedge C1-C4

The contribution is delimited by four clauses:

- C1 (LAYER AND COST): operates purely at the local learning-rate-bound layer, reusing only quantities already available to the client; no change to the per-round model-update exchange, aggregation, model architecture, or privacy contract, and zero added per-round communication (the optional offline fit of the shared slope uses only coarse, secure-aggregated, bin-level validation summaries, broadcast like any other hyperparameter).
- C2 (OBSERVABLE COVARIATE): conditions the bound schedule on an observable, privacy-preserving heterogeneity covariate d_i computed from already-available quantities.
- C3 (STRUCTURED MAP): uses a structured monotone map of the covariate - a shared low-dimensional functional form (a single-slope default, optionally refined by hierarchical partial pooling) - which is neither a single global scalar (it varies across clients through d_i) nor an unpooled per-client free parameter (its parameters are shared across clients).
- C4 (EXACT COLLAPSE): provably recovers the base method update-for-update at the covariate-blind default (theta=0), CI-gated at E_collapse <= 1e-6.

The central novelty claim, established in Section 3 Question 4, is that No prior line covers more than two of C1-C4.

### 2.6 Analysis summary

MONOBAND is a latent, metric-hidden blind spot in the dynamic-bound pFL line; RHEOSTAT is a minimal, host-flexible, privacy-preserving promotion of one global scalar into a covariate-conditional monotone map with a provable exact-collapse safety guarantee. The remainder of the document develops this into a complete, falsifiable research proposal.

---

## Section 3. Novel Research Idea (5q)

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

In personalized federated learning that uses a dynamic bound of the learning rate, can replacing the single global bound-tightening (adaptive-retention) scalar - applied identically to every client - with a structured, monotone map (a shared functional form, optionally hierarchically partially pooled) of an observable per-client heterogeneity covariate d_i recover the accuracy of the most distribution-skewed clients without regressing pooled accuracy and while provably collapsing to the base method at its covariate-blind default?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

Solving this matters to the FL research community for five connected reasons.

P1 - It exposes and closes a latent design axis. The dynamic learning-rate bound has been studied as a temporal, optimization-driven device (transition from Adam-like to SGD-like behavior) and never as a heterogeneity-conditional personalization lever. Reframing the bound as a function of an observable covariate d_i opens a new, orthogonal axis of personalization that sits underneath the model and the regularizer, and that composes with existing pFL methods rather than competing with them.

P2 - It directly serves the clients FL most wants to help. The whole motivation for FL under Non-IID data is to serve clients whose distributions diverge from the aggregate. Pooled accuracy rewards the majority and hides tail collapse. By making divergence-stratified, worst-group accuracy the primary metric and by raising the high-heterogeneity tail, the work advances fairness and per-client equity, which are first-class goals in the FL literature [19], [23].

P3 - It is immediately deployable because of exact collapse. The proposal is not "replace your optimizer" but "wrap your existing bound in a covariate-conditional schedule that is provably identical to what you run today when the conditioning is switched off." Practitioners can adopt it with a zero-regression guarantee (E_collapse <= 1e-6), shadow-deploy it, audit it, and disable it instantly. This lowers the barrier between a research result and production use.

P4 - It is host-flexible and therefore broadly reusable. The scalar-to-map promotion applies directly to any optimizer that exposes a CLIENT-SIDE learning-rate bound or step schedule - AdaBound-style local optimizers [5] (the primary host) and the client-side inner learning rate of meta-learning methods such as Per-FedAvg [9] - where the C1 local-only, zero-added-communication property is preserved exactly. The same conceptual move (condition the schedule on an observable heterogeneity covariate) is conjectured to extend to server-side adaptive schedules such as FedAdam/FedOpt [4], but that extension would condition on aggregated rather than purely local signals and is therefore scoped as future work rather than claimed here.

P5 - It contributes a transferable methodological pattern. Recasting a global optimizer hyperparameter as a monotone, covariate-conditional function (optionally partially pooled) of an observable heterogeneity covariate, with a provable exact-collapse identity, is a template that future work can apply to other global FL hyperparameters (proximal mu, mixing alpha, client-sampling temperatures). Demonstrating it cleanly on the learning-rate bound establishes the pattern and the evaluation protocol (divergence-stratified metrics, monotone-dose and covariate-scramble falsifiers) that such work should adopt.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

The problem resists naive solutions for six reasons; the sixth is the binding obstacle.

O1 - The per-client optimum is entangled. The best adaptive-retention for a client depends jointly on its heterogeneity, its local sample count, its label balance, and the training stage. A straightforward "give skewed clients a bigger learning rate" rule conflates these axes and can destabilize clients that are skewed but data-poor. The method must isolate the heterogeneity dependence while remaining stable across the other axes.

O2 - Data poverty forbids unpooled per-client tuning. Many clients hold only tens to hundreds of examples. Fitting a free per-client retention parameter would overfit local validation noise and generalize worse than the global scalar it replaces. This is why a structured estimator that SHARES statistical strength across clients is required - a shared single-slope map by default, or hierarchical partial pooling for finer control - so that data-poor clients shrink toward the global default and only data-rich, clearly-skewed clients (or bins) move far from it.

O3 - The covariate must be observable and privacy-preserving. The conditioning signal d_i has to be computable from quantities a client already holds (its local model and the downloaded global model, or its label histogram and the global prior), because adding new uploads would change the communication and privacy contract. Under secure aggregation the server cannot see individual updates at all, so any server-side divergence computation is unavailable; the design must keep d_i a client-side quantity.

O4 - Heterogeneity is multi-axis and must be reduced to a faithful scalar proxy. Divergence has label-skew, feature-shift, and quantity-skew components. Collapsing these into one normalized d_i that correlates with the true client-conditional optimum, and that is comparable across datasets (so the same monotone map transfers), is non-trivial and must be validated, not assumed.

O5 - Lift and exact collapse must hold simultaneously. The method must improve the skewed tail AND guarantee that, at its default, it reproduces the base method to numerical tolerance. These pull in opposite directions: expressive enough to help, yet provably inert when switched off. Achieving an exact (not approximate) collapse requires the map to pass through the base setting algebraically (g_theta(0)=0, theta=0), not merely to be "close."

O6 - BINDING OBSTACLE: metric blindness. The dominant evaluation metric - pooled mean accuracy - cannot see the harm being fixed, because over-constraining the skewed tail and under-constraining the near-IID core cancel in the mean. A method that genuinely helps the tail can look like noise on the headline number, and a global scalar tuned to maximize the mean actively sits at the cancellation point. Naive benchmarking therefore neither reveals the problem nor rewards the solution. Overcoming O6 requires a different measurement instrument (divergence-stratified, worst-group accuracy) and falsifiers (monotone-dose, covariate-scramble) that tie any observed gain to the heterogeneity mechanism rather than to generic regularization. This is why the gap stayed open: not because the fix is intricate, but because the field was not looking with a metric that could detect it.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

Prior work clusters into six near-miss families; each fails at least two of the four wedge clauses C1-C4, and a single conceptual barrier explains the omission.

NM1 - Adaptive optimizers with global bounds (AdaBound [5], Adam [6], AMSGrad [7], server-side FedAdam/FedOpt [4]). These adapt the step to the per-coordinate gradient second moment - an OPTIMIZATION signal - and use a single global bound schedule. They never condition on a client's DISTRIBUTIONAL divergence d_i, never use a structured monotone map of a heterogeneity covariate, and have no concept of collapsing to a simpler base (they are the base). Misses C2, C3, C4. NM1 is the exact-collapse target.

NM2 - Local-adaptivity and normalization (FedNova [17], per-client local-epoch or gradient-norm scaling). These vary effective local progress by quantity or gradient norm, not by distributional divergence, are not a structured monotone map of an observable heterogeneity covariate, and offer no exact-collapse identity to a bound-based base. Misses C2, C3, C4.

NM3 - Personalized FL via model interpolation or regularization (pFedMe [8], Ditto [11], APFL [10], Per-FedAvg [9], FedRep [22]). Two sub-cases both fall short. (a) pFedMe, Ditto, Per-FedAvg, and FedRep personalize the MODEL or a regularization coefficient (lambda, mu, a meta learning rate) that is a single GLOBAL scalar tuned for the federation, operate at the model/regularizer layer rather than the learning-rate-bound layer, and provide no collapse-to-base gate - satisfying none of C1-C4. (b) APFL [10] is the sharpest near-miss because its mixing weight alpha_i is genuinely per-client and adapted online; but alpha_i is updated from the local gradient correlation between the personalized and global models (an optimization signal), NOT conditioned on the observable cross-federation heterogeneity covariate d_i (not C2); it is an unpooled per-client free scalar, not a structured monotone map of that covariate (not C3); it acts at the model-interpolation layer, not the bound layer (not C1); and it has no exact-collapse identity (not C4). In either sub-case the family covers none of the four clauses.

NM4 - Clustered FL and client selection (IFCA [18], FAVOR [15] with double Q-learning [27], FedMA [16]). These DO use a heterogeneity signal, but to GROUP, SELECT, or MATCH clients discretely at the aggregation/protocol layer, not to set a per-client continuous monotone learning-rate-bound map; they impose hard cluster assignments rather than partial pooling and they alter the protocol. Misses C1, C3, and typically C4.

NM5 - Drift-correction and proximal methods (SCAFFOLD [3], FedProx [2]). These reduce client drift via control variates or a proximal term whose strength is a global scalar (mu), not a per-client monotone function of observed d_i; SCAFFOLD additionally communicates control variates, changing the comms/privacy contract. FedProx misses C2, C3, C4; SCAFFOLD misses C1, C2, C3.

NM6 - Federated hyperparameter optimization (FedEx [26]). This searches for good hyperparameters, including learning rates, over a shared configuration by black-box weight-sharing search, rather than CONDITIONING the bound on a per-client observable covariate through a structured monotone map, and it offers no exact-collapse identity. Misses C2, C3, C4.

Across all six families, No prior line covers more than two of C1-C4. The barrier is twofold and explains the omission directly. First, the learning-rate bound was historically conceived as an optimization stabilizer - something that adapts to gradient scale and guarantees convergence - so the idea of conditioning it on a client's distributional heterogeneity was outside the frame. Second, and decisively, the only metric that would have revealed the benefit (divergence-stratified, worst-group accuracy) is not the field's headline number; under pooled accuracy the harm of a global bound cancels, so there was neither a signal that something was wrong nor a reward for fixing it. RHEOSTAT differs by (a) moving the conditioning to the bound layer with a covariate that is observable and privacy-preserving, (b) using a structured monotone map of the covariate (optionally partially pooled) instead of a scalar or an unpooled per-client parameter, and (c) guaranteeing exact collapse to the base method, which makes the intervention safe to evaluate and deploy.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

Method. Let the base pFL method run, at each client i and local step t (indexed from t = 1, so the bounds are well defined), an AdaBound-style local optimizer that clips the per-coordinate Adam step into the interval [eta_l(t), eta_u(t)] with eta_l(t) = eta_inf * (1 - 1/(gamma * t + 1)) and eta_u(t) = eta_inf * (1 + 1/(gamma * t)), where gamma is the base schedule's convergence-speed constant (gamma = 1 - beta2 in canonical AdaBound) and both bounds converge to the terminal step eta_inf [5]. RHEOSTAT introduces a per-client adaptive-retention factor b_i = b(d_i) >= 1 that stretches the bound time constant: eta_l(t; b_i) = eta_inf * (1 - 1/(gamma * (t / b_i) + 1)) and eta_u(t; b_i) = eta_inf * (1 + 1/(gamma * (t / b_i))). The resulting bound width eta_u - eta_l = eta_inf * (b_i/(gamma * t) + b_i/(gamma * t + b_i)) is strictly monotone increasing in b_i (asymptotically proportional to b_i/(gamma * t)), so a larger b_i keeps the client in the wide-bound adaptive regime longer; at b_i = 1 the schedule reduces exactly to the chosen base schedule. We set b(d_i) = clamp_{>= 1}(1 + g_theta(d_i)) with g_theta monotone non-decreasing and g_theta(0) = 0.

Covariate. d_i is a client-side, normalized divergence between client i and the federation, computed from quantities the client already holds. Primary form: model drift d_i = || w_i_local - w_global ||_2 / (|| w_global ||_2 + eps) after local adaptation. Alternative form: label-profile divergence d_i = TV(p_i, p_bar), the total-variation distance between client i's empirical label histogram p_i and the global label prior p_bar, normalized to [0,1]. d_i is normalized to a common range (TV in [0,1], or capped by d_max) for cross-dataset comparability. No new value is uploaded; b(d_i) is applied locally inside the client optimizer.

Estimator and partial pooling. g_theta is a low-dimensional monotone map (linear g_theta(d) = theta * d with theta >= 0, or a monotone spline / isotonic regression). The DEPLOYABLE DEFAULT is the fixed one-slope form b(d_i) = clamp_{>= 1}(1 + theta * d_i) with a single shared slope theta tuned once as an ordinary hyperparameter; this is fully client-side, adds zero per-round communication, and is the form to which the exact-collapse gate (theta = 0) applies directly. As an OPTIONAL refinement, theta or K per-bin offsets may be fit by hierarchical partial pooling so that per-bin offsets share a global prior, data-poor clients shrink toward the global default (b = 1), and only data-rich, clearly-skewed bins move appreciably; this fit consumes only coarse, secure-aggregated, bin-level validation summaries (for example the mean validation accuracy per heterogeneity bin), computed offline and infrequently, never raw per-client d_i and never an extra per-round model exchange, and the resulting small shared parameter vector is broadcast like any other hyperparameter. Partial pooling is therefore reconciled with the privacy contract: the only cross-client signal is coarse, aggregated, and occasional.

Exact-collapse gate. At theta = 0, g_theta is identically zero, so b(d_i) = 1 for all clients and the schedule equals the base bound exactly, reproducing every local update. We assert E_collapse = max over clients and rounds of the per-update parameter L-infinity difference between RHEOSTAT(theta=0) and the base method, and require E_collapse <= 1e-6 in continuous integration. The identity is an exact algebraic zero by construction.

Datasets. CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 [25] partitioned by Dirichlet(alpha) with alpha in {0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 1.0, 100} to sweep heterogeneity from severe skew (small alpha, large d_i) to near-IID (alpha = 100, d_i -> 0) [28]; the LEAF datasets FEMNIST and Shakespeare [24] for natural, writer/speaker-based Non-IID; and Tiny-ImageNet (Dirichlet-partitioned) for a harder vision setting. Host methods (both client-side, so C1 holds exactly): AdaBound-style pFL as the primary exact-collapse target, and the client-side inner learning rate of Per-FedAvg [9] as a second host to test generality; the server-side FedAdam/FedOpt [4] extension is noted as future work because it would condition on aggregated rather than local signals.

Baselines. B1 Local-only (no FL); B2 FedAvg [1]; B3 FedProx [2]; B4 SCAFFOLD [3]; B5 base dynamic-bound pFL with a single global bound (the EXACT-COLLAPSE TARGET); B6 unpooled per-client bound (free b_i, no pooling) to ablate C3; B7 covariate-scrambled bound (b assigned via a random permutation of d_i) as a falsifier control; B8 RHEOSTAT (ours). An additional reference oracle B9 grid-tunes b_i per client on held-out data; B9 is an impractical, compute-intensive upper bound (its per-client grid search is not deployable) reported only to bound the achievable gain.

Metrics. PRIMARY: worst-20 percent (high-d_i tail) client accuracy and accuracy within the top heterogeneity bin. SECONDARY: the divergence-stratified accuracy curve and its slope (accuracy versus d_i), expected to rise/flatten at high d_i under RHEOSTAT; the fairness gap (accuracy disparity between the low-d_i and high-d_i deciles, and the cross-client accuracy variance); pooled/mean accuracy, which must NOT regress (within +/- 0.3 points); communication overhead, which must be 0 percent (verified, since the protocol is unchanged); and E_collapse for the collapse gate.

Ablations. A1 covariate choice: model-drift versus label-profile-TV versus gradient-norm proxy for d_i. A2 pooling strength: full pooling (= global scalar) versus partial pooling versus no pooling (per-client), to isolate the value of C3. A3 monotone versus unconstrained map, to show the monotone constraint generalizes better under data poverty. A4 number of bins K in {3, 5, 10}. A5 dose DIRECTION: retain-adaptivity-longer for high-d_i versus the reversed assignment, a direct test of the mechanism's sign. A6 host method: AdaBound-pFL versus Per-FedAvg client-side inner learning rate, for generality (both preserve C1).

Falsifiability tests. T1 IID null: at alpha = 100 (d_i -> 0), RHEOSTAT must match B5 with no improvement and no regression, consistent with exact collapse. T2 monotone dose: the tail improvement must increase monotonically with federation heterogeneity; a flat or reversed trend refutes the mechanism. T3 covariate scramble: permuting d_i across clients (B7) must destroy the gain; if a scrambled covariate works as well as the true one, the gain is not from heterogeneity conditioning and the claim is refuted.

Hypotheses (numerical ranges are conjectured, with no preliminary results, and are stated to be falsified or confirmed). H1: RHEOSTAT improves worst-20 percent high-d_i accuracy by a conjectured +2 to +8 points over B5 at small alpha, with no regression at large alpha. H2: pooled accuracy does not regress, staying within +/- 0.3 points of B5. H3: the low-d_i-minus-high-d_i fairness gap shrinks by a conjectured 15 to 40 percent. H4: the tail gain grows monotonically with heterogeneity (passes T2). H5: covariate scrambling removes at least a conjectured 80 percent of the gain (passes T3). H6: the exact-collapse gate holds, E_collapse <= 1e-6 at theta = 0, deterministically and by construction.

Expected outcomes. We expect RHEOSTAT to lift the skewed-client tail and shrink the fairness gap on the Dirichlet sweeps and on FEMNIST/Shakespeare, to leave pooled accuracy statistically unchanged, to add zero per-round communication, to pass the exact-collapse gate, and to be refuted-or-confirmed cleanly by T1-T3. A negative result (flat dose, scramble-insensitive, or tail not improved) would itself be informative, establishing that a global dynamic bound is already adequate and that the heterogeneity-conditional axis is not exploitable - either way, the divergence-stratified evaluation protocol and the exact-collapse construction are contributed as reusable tools.

Responsible AI and Data-Governance Considerations. F1 (privacy surface): the default keeps d_i a CLIENT-SIDE quantity that is never uploaded, so RHEOSTAT adds no inference surface; if any deployment chooses to report d_i for monitoring, it must use secure aggregation, calibrated differential-privacy noise, and a minimum-cohort floor (n_i >= 10) before a divergence is computed or logged. F2 (demographic-proxy governance): client heterogeneity d_i can correlate with protected attributes (for example a hospital's patient mix), so a governance review and a cohort-aggregated covariate alternative are required before any non-research deployment. F3 (auditability and disable switch): per-client and per-round values of d_i and b(d_i) are logged, and the exact-collapse setting (theta = 0) is an explicit, instantaneous disable switch for incident response. F4 (scientific integrity): all numerical hypotheses above are marked conjectured with no preliminary results, and the E_collapse unit test is committed for public reproducibility. F5 (fairness floor): because theta = 0 reproduces the base method exactly, RHEOSTAT has a provable do-no-harm default and is shadow-deployable; any active configuration (theta > 0) is promoted from shadow to live only after held-out validation confirms no pooled-accuracy regression, so the covariate-blind default is a floor rather than a risk.

---

## Section 4. References and Evidence

Every reference below is cited in Sections 1-3. The proposal builds on real, published FL, personalization, and optimization work; the evidence for the gap is structural (a single global bound schedule in the dynamic-bound pFL line) and is tested by the falsifiers T1-T3 and the exact-collapse gate.
